---
title: "Caching"
description: "Understand layer caching and cache invalidation in Go template builds."
---

# Caching

Template caching in E2B is conceptually similar to Docker layer caching. Each template builder step creates a new layer, and matching layers can be reused across builds instead of rebuilt from scratch.

## Go-specific behavior

- `template.SkipCache()` invalidates caching from the next template instruction onward
- `BuildOptions.BasicBuildOptions.SkipCache` skips cache usage for the whole build request
- `Copy(..., &struct{ ForceUpload bool }{ForceUpload: true})` forces the source files to be uploaded again instead of reusing the existing file-upload cache
- if a copy source is already present and `ForceUpload` is `false`, the current Go SDK skips the upload and reuses the cached source archive
- if you call `SkipCache()` before selecting the base image, the current Go serializer treats that as forcing the whole template

## Layer caching

Builder calls such as `Copy(...)`, `RunCmd(...)`, and `SetEnvs(...)` create ordered template layers. When the instruction and its inputs match a previously built layer, E2B can reuse it.

This is especially useful for large templates with expensive install steps.

## Invalidate cache from the next instruction

Use `SkipCache()` when you want the next instruction and all following instructions to rebuild.

```go
package main

import (
	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

func main() {
	template := e2b.Template(nil).
		FromBaseImage().
		SkipCache().
		RunCmd(`echo "Hello, World!"`)

	_ = template
}
```

This pattern is the Go equivalent of partial cache invalidation.

## Invalidate the whole build

Use `BuildOptions.BasicBuildOptions.SkipCache` when you want the entire build request to bypass cached layers.

```go
package main

import (
	"context"

	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

func main() {
	ctx := context.Background()
	template := e2b.Template(nil).FromBaseImage()

	_, _ = e2b.Build(ctx, template, "my-template", &e2b.BuildOptions{
		BasicBuildOptions: e2b.BasicBuildOptions{
			SkipCache: true,
		},
	})
}
```

This is different from `template.SkipCache()`: it applies to the whole build request, not just the next template step.

## File upload caching

For `Copy(...)` steps, the Go SDK computes a content-based `filesHash` and checks whether those files were already uploaded for the build.

If the files are already cached and `ForceUpload` is not set, the current SDK skips the upload and reuses the existing file archive.

```go
package main

import (
	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

func main() {
	template := e2b.Template(nil).
		FromBaseImage().
		Copy("config.json", "/app/config.json", nil)

	_ = template
}
```

To invalidate both the affected template step and the file-upload reuse for that copy source, set `ForceUpload`.

```go
package main

import (
	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

func main() {
	template := e2b.Template(nil).
		FromBaseImage().
		Copy("config.json", "/app/config.json", &struct{ ForceUpload bool }{
			ForceUpload: true,
		})

	_ = template
}
```

## Reuse the same template definition across variants

Caching helps when you build several template variants from the same definition, such as different CPU or memory configurations.

```go
package main

import (
	"context"

	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

func main() {
	ctx := context.Background()
	template := e2b.Template(nil).
		FromBaseImage().
		RunCmd("go build ./...")

	_, _ = e2b.Build(ctx, template, "my-template-2cpu-2gb", &e2b.BuildOptions{
		BasicBuildOptions: e2b.BasicBuildOptions{
			CpuCount: 2,
			MemoryMB: 2048,
		},
	})

	_, _ = e2b.Build(ctx, template, "my-template-1cpu-4gb", &e2b.BuildOptions{
		BasicBuildOptions: e2b.BasicBuildOptions{
			CpuCount: 1,
			MemoryMB: 4096,
		},
	})
}
```

## Optimize build times

- keep frequently changing `Copy(...)` steps toward the end of the template
- keep expensive install steps early when they rarely change
- use `ForceUpload` only when you actually need to invalidate the copy-source upload cache
- use `BuildOptions.SkipCache` sparingly, because it disables reuse for the whole build

For the builder APIs behind these cache behaviors, see [Defining Template](/docs/template/defining-template) and the [Go SDK Template reference](/docs/sdk-reference/go-sdk/template).
